learning_outcomes.description: Draw on a range of interdisciplinary skills to analyse works of writing, music, and art.
Demonstrate facility with different forms of formal and contextual analysis through application of analysis skills to written, sonic, and visual objects.
Demonstrate the application of close-reading skills to the interpretation of a primary source.
Demonstrate an understanding of AI-prompting and the advantages and disadvantages of using AI.
Conceptualise the skills pertinent to formal and contextual analysis of primary sources in an interdisciplinary humanities context.

assessments.task_description:
Each tutorial will be accompanied by a worksheet that you will receive in class that day, and will submit before leaving the tutorial. The worksheets can be filled in throughout the tutorial, and will be a vital part of participation in each week's class.

As per UQ Policy, your tutor may request that you submit a doctors' note or other documentation as part of the makeup process for an absence.

This assessment task evaluates students’ abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: This assessment includes the submission of two pieces of work. Students will be given a passage from one of the primary sources studied. The first piece of work that must be submitted is an AI-generated commentary on the meaning of the set text in which students will demonstrate an understanding of AI prompting (20% of overall grade). The second piece of work is a 1000-word critique written by the student of the AI-generated commentary. This critique must demonstrate the student's close reading of the relevant passage and indicate where the AI-generated commentary has correctly or incorrectly interpreted the set text (80% of the overall grade).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
21/09/2026 -

In Class & Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard
assessments.task_description:
THIS IS AN IN-CLASS ASSESSMENT: You will present a musical analysis of a favorite song, using the tools and terminology covered in the previous weeks. Your presentation will be 3-4 minutes in length, and will describe why this song is a favorite of yours through your close analysis of it. You will be assessed on your use of musical terminology, close analysis ability, and presentation skills, including public speaking and PowerPoint slides. You are required to submit your script and slides via Turnitin as well.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In Class, and Submit materials via Turnitin through Blackboard
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final exam will consist of essay questions based on the course images, lectures, readings and tutorials. Students will select essay questions from a number of questions on the exam paper. All questions will relate to the formal and historical aspects of works of art that have been discussed in class. The essay questions test a student's understanding of the research methodologies employed by Art Historians through their ability to visually analyse the formal and design aspects of works of art and to comprehend the works’ significance in historical context. Sample questions will be provided and discussed in advance during tutorials. 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Introduction to the Course

Introduction to the course. Lecturers: Dr Anne Levitsky, Dr David Kearns, and Dr Ben Pollitt.

Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: Introduction to the Course Introduction to the course. Lecturers: Dr Anne Levitsky, Dr David Kearns, and Dr Ben Pollitt.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Intention

Lecturer: Dr David Kearns.

Students will be introduced to the study of authorial intention as elaborated by Quentin Skinner, with a key focus on whether Mary Wollstonecraft can be described as a feminist.

Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Intention Lecturer: Dr David Kearns. Students will be introduced to the study of authorial intention as elaborated by Quentin Skinner, with a key focus on whether Mary Wollstonecraft can be described as a feminist.

learning_activities.topic:
Tutorial 1: Intention

Tutor: Dr David Kearns

In-class discussion will focus on Mary Wollstonecraft, ‘A Vindication of the Rights of Woman’, in Sylvana Tomaselli, ed., A Vindication of the Rights of Men and a Vindication of the Rights of Woman (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2012 [1792]), 65-294.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Tutorial 1: Intention Tutor: Dr David Kearns In-class discussion will focus on Mary Wollstonecraft, ‘A Vindication of the Rights of Woman’, in Sylvana Tomaselli, ed., A Vindication of the Rights of Men and a Vindication of the Rights of Woman (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2012 [1792]), 65-294.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Language

Lecturer: Dr David Kearns.

Students will be introduced to the study of texts as exhibiting languages, as developed by J. G. A. Pocock, with a key focus on whether Edmund Burke inaugurated conservatism.

Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Language Lecturer: Dr David Kearns. Students will be introduced to the study of texts as exhibiting languages, as developed by J. G. A. Pocock, with a key focus on whether Edmund Burke inaugurated conservatism.

learning_activities.topic:
Tutorial 3: Language

Tutor: Dr David Kearns

In-class discussion will focus on Edmund Burke, ‘Reflections on the Revolution in France’, in L. G. Mitchell, ed., The Writings and Speeches of Edmund Burke, Volume 8: The French Revolution: 1790-1794 (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1989 [1790]), 53-293

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Tutorial 3: Language Tutor: Dr David Kearns In-class discussion will focus on Edmund Burke, ‘Reflections on the Revolution in France’, in L. G. Mitchell, ed., The Writings and Speeches of Edmund Burke, Volume 8: The French Revolution: 1790-1794 (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1989 [1790]), 53-293

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Persona

Lecturer: Dr David Kearns. 

Students will be introduced to the study of persona and, in particular, the role of office in early modern England via Conal Condren, with a key focus on the alleged authoritarianism or liberalism of Thomas Hobbes.

Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4: Persona Lecturer: Dr David Kearns. Students will be introduced to the study of persona and, in particular, the role of office in early modern England via Conal Condren, with a key focus on the alleged authoritarianism or liberalism of Thomas Hobbes.

learning_activities.topic:
Tutorial 4: Persona

Tutor: Dr David Kearns

In-class discussion will focus on Thomas Hobbes, The Clarendon Editions of the Works of Thomas Hobbes, Volumes 4 and 5: Leviathan: The English and Latin Texts (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2012 [1651])

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Tutorial 4: Persona Tutor: Dr David Kearns In-class discussion will focus on Thomas Hobbes, The Clarendon Editions of the Works of Thomas Hobbes, Volumes 4 and 5: Leviathan: The English and Latin Texts (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2012 [1651])

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Institutions

Lecturer: Dr David Kearns.

Students will be introduced to the study of institutional vocabularies, following the work of Ian Hunter and David Saunders, with a key focus on the history of free speech and authorship.

Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Institutions Lecturer: Dr David Kearns. Students will be introduced to the study of institutional vocabularies, following the work of Ian Hunter and David Saunders, with a key focus on the history of free speech and authorship.
